children.value: Why we decided to take a feature-based approach

children.label: Why we decided to take a feature-based approach

children.value: Rather than feeding the raw 32-slot allocation time series to the neural network (which caused training instability due to sparsity, variable sequence lengths, and the curse of dimensionality), we extract a fixed-size vector of 40 behavioral summary statistics per session. This approach eliminates variable-length padding issues, replaces sparse integer inputs with continuous, bounded features, ensures identical feature computation for simulated and real participant data (no domain shift) and thus reduces the neural network’s task from “learn to extract behavioral patterns from raw sequences” to “learn to map extracted patterns to parameters” - which also best aligns with the core research focus.

Furthermore, this approach simplifies the integration of multimodal data while maintaining the same network architecture; and - even more interestingly - it potentially allows for generalization to various tasks rather than being limited to a single paradigm.
All 40 features satisfy the following requirements (to ensure traceability and computational stability):
: No discrete or unbounded values that could destabilize training

children.html: {gt}\{\mathbf{g}_t\}{gt​}

children.value: and schedule, the features are identical
: The same extraction code operates on both simulated agent output and real participant data (to avoid preprocessing differences that could create domain shift)
: Each feature is designed to capture a specific aspect of behavior that distinguishes between parameter values, based on prior sensitivity analysis (see
, associated behavioral features in the parameter definition section)
The 40 specified features are organized into 8 groups, each targeting different aspects of behavior:
Net entropy, slots occupied, stacking fraction
Angular error, defense rate, tracking error
Oddball shift ratio, fraction followed, recovery speed
Recovery slope, post-switch angular error, entropy peak
Learned cue offset, cue defense rate difference
Defense rate slope, angular error slope
See Appendix A for complete feature definitions
This set of features primarily identifies
Mean entropy of net allocation (bits) — higher = more spread
Mean stacking depth (max nets in one slot)
Fraction of trials with ≥2 nets in any slot
Mean circular variance of allocation (0 = concentrated, 1 = uniform)
Entropy difference: second half minus first half (learning effect)
Mean angular distance between allocation center and actual outcome (°)
Fraction of outcome trials where ≥1 net covers the attacked slot
Defense rate in last 3 episodes minus first 3 episodes
Mean distance from current allocation to

On a behavioral level, this set of features reflect how participants react to oddballs. Consequently, they reflect belief stability, how well participants identified the underlying latent structures (if a participant knows, that an environmental transition occures all ~20 trials, this participant would learn to ignore single oddball events within episodes without getting highly irritated - oddballs are not longer unexpected and suprising, they do not longer foster faster belief updating, e.g. oddball-sensitivity). Thus, the oddball reaction features primarily identify noise_reactivity.
Entropy increase 1 trial after oddball vs. baseline
Allocation shift (°) from trial before to trial after oddball
Mean allocation distance from pre-oddball position over 3 post-oddball trials
Fraction of oddballs where allocation moves toward the oddball direction
This set of features capture how participants react to environmental transitions (e.g. episode-transitions, 120° shifts). primarily identifies belief_momentum
Angular error at trial 1 after episode transition
Angular error at trial 3 after transition
Angular error at trial 5 after transition
Linear slope of angular error decrease (trials 1–5 post-transition)
Maximum entropy in first 3 post-transition trials
Trials until entropy returns to within-episode baseline
Does recovery speed improve across transitions?
Block 5: Cue Differentiation (4 features) → h_switch × noise_reactivity

children.label: Block 5: Cue Differentiation (4 features) → h_switch × noise_reactivity

children.value: Estimated angular offset between Cue A and Cue B allocations (true: 70°)
Variability of cue offset across episodes
Defense rate difference between cues (should be ~0 with good learning)
Mean angular shift between consecutive trials (behavioral volatility)
Fraction of trials with >45° shift (z-flip marker)
Block 7: Omission Reaction (2 features) → control features

children.label: Block 7: Omission Reaction (2 features) → control features

children.value: Allocation shift after omission trials (should be ~0 for normative agent)
